stem: A 55-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) presents with massive hematemesis of bright red blood. He is confused and diaphoretic. Abdominal exam shows ascites and splenomegaly.Vital SignsBP 78/45HR 128RR 28Temp 37.2°CSpO₂ 90%LabsHgb 6.8 g/dLPlatelets 62 K/μLINR 2.1Cr 1.8 mg/dL What is the immediate management triad for acute variceal hemorrhage?

choices.text: IV PPI bolus + EGD within 24 hours + oral H. pylori therapy
IV octreotide + prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) + emergent EGD with band ligation within 12 hours
Fresh frozen plasma to correct INR before any intervention
Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) as initial management

explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute variceal hemorrhage management triad: (1) IV octreotide (50 μg bolus → 50 μg/h infusion; splanchnic vasoconstriction reduces portal pressure), (2) Prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV daily x 7 days; reduces mortality by 20%), (3) Emergent EGD with band ligation within 12 hours. Restrictive transfusion (target Hgb 7-8) — over-transfusion worsens portal pressure and rebleeding.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Cirrhosis + massive hematemesis = variceal bleed until proven otherwise
- Octreotide reduces splanchnic blood flow → reduces portal pressure → reduces variceal bleeding
- Antibiotics are CRITICAL: cirrhotic patients with UGIB have 20% risk of bacterial infection; prophylaxis reduces mortality
- Band ligation is superior to sclerotherapy for esophageal varices (lower rebleeding, fewer complications)

Evidence: Variceal Hemorrhage - StatPearls - Garcia-Tsao G, et al. StatPearls. NBK470525. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- IV PPI bolus + EGD within 24 hours + oral H. pylori therapy: This is the management of PEPTIC ULCER bleeding, not variceal hemorrhage. PPI has no role in variceal bleeding (different pathophysiology — portal hypertension, not acid injury). Variceal bleed requires octreotide, antibiotics, and EGD within 12 hours (not 24).
- TIPS placement as first-line intervention: TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) is a rescue intervention for variceal bleeding that fails endoscopic therapy. It is not first-line — endoscopic band ligation controls bleeding in 80-90% of cases. TIPS carries risks of encephalopathy worsening and liver failure.
- Fresh frozen plasma to correct INR before any intervention: The coagulopathy of cirrhosis is complex: INR does NOT accurately reflect bleeding risk in cirrhosis (balanced hemostatic defect). FFP transfusion is volume-expanding and can worsen portal pressure. Do NOT delay EGD to correct INR. Transfuse platelets only if <50 K and actively bleeding.
- Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) as initial management: Balloon tamponade is a BRIDGE therapy for life-threatening variceal bleeding that cannot be controlled endoscopically, not a first-line intervention. Maximum duration is 24 hours due to complications (esophageal rupture, aspiration, pressure necrosis). First-line is octreotide + antibiotics + EGD with banding.

Reference: Variceal Hemorrhage - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470525/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 72-year-old man presents with painless bright red blood per rectum (hematochezia) that started 3 hours ago. He has passed 3 large bloody bowel movements. He takes aspirin 81 mg daily. No abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting.Vital SignsBP 128/75HR 90RR 16Temp 36.9°CSpO₂ 98%LabsHgb 11.2 g/dLBUN 18 mg/dLCr 1.0 mg/dLPlatelets 195 K/μL What is the most common cause of acute lower GI bleeding in adults, and what is the initial diagnostic approach?

choices.text: Hemorrhoids; no further workup needed if hemorrhoids found on exam
Colorectal cancer; urgent colonoscopy within 6 hours
Diverticulosis; resuscitate, then colonoscopy within 24 hours after bowel prep

explanation:
Correct Answer: Diverticulosis is the #1 cause of acute lower GI bleeding (30-40%), typically presenting as painless, large-volume hematochezia. Colonoscopy within 24 hours (after adequate bowel prep) is the recommended initial diagnostic and therapeutic approach per ACG guidelines. Most diverticular bleeds (70-80%) stop spontaneously.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Painless hematochezia in elderly = diverticulosis until proven otherwise
- BUN:Cr ratio normal (18:1) — argues against upper GI source
- Hemodynamically stable = time for proper bowel prep before colonoscopy
- Colonoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic (can treat active diverticular bleed with clips/cautery)
- ACG 2023 guidelines recommend colonoscopy within 24 hours for acute LGIB

Evidence: Lower GI Bleeding - StatPearls - Strate LL, et al. StatPearls. NBK448126. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Hemorrhoids; no further workup needed if hemorrhoids found on exam: Hemorrhoids cause LGIB but typically present with small-volume bright red blood on toilet paper or coating stool. Large-volume hematochezia requiring multiple bloody bowel movements is unlikely from hemorrhoids alone. Even if hemorrhoids are present, colonoscopy is needed to rule out a more proximal source.
- Colorectal cancer; urgent colonoscopy within 6 hours: Colorectal cancer accounts for <5% of acute LGIB and typically presents with occult bleeding or chronic iron deficiency anemia, not acute large-volume hematochezia. The 24-hour colonoscopy window (not 6 hours) allows proper bowel prep, which improves visualization and diagnostic yield.
- Ischemic colitis; emergent CT angiography: Ischemic colitis presents with abdominal PAIN (crampy left-sided) followed by bloody diarrhea — this patient has painless hematochezia. CT angiography is indicated for suspected acute mesenteric ischemia (arterial occlusion) or when bleeding is too brisk for colonoscopy, not as the initial approach for stable LGIB.
- Angiodysplasia; immediate mesenteric angiography: Angiodysplasia accounts for 5-10% of LGIB and is more common in the right colon. While angiography can identify and treat active bleeding, colonoscopy remains the first-line diagnostic approach for hemodynamically stable patients. Angiography requires active bleeding rate ≥0.5-1 mL/min to detect the source.

Reference: LGIB - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448126/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 24-year-old man presents with 18 hours of abdominal pain that began periumbilically and has migrated to the right lower quadrant. He has had one episode of vomiting and anorexia. He is lying still and grimaces when the stretcher is bumped.Vital SignsBP 128/78HR 96RR 18Temp 38.2°CSpO₂ 99%ExamRLQ Point tenderness at McBurney's pointRovsing sign Positive (RLQ pain with LLQ palpation)Psoas sign Positive (pain with right hip extension)Rebound Present in RLQLabsWBC 14.8 K/μL with left shiftUA Trace WBCs (no bacteria) What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute appendicitis classic presentation: periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ (visceral → somatic transition) + anorexia + low-grade fever + leukocytosis. Physical exam: McBurney's point tenderness (1/3 distance from ASIS to umbilicus), Rovsing sign (RLQ pain with LLQ palpation), psoas sign (retrocecal appendix), obturator sign (pelvic appendix). Trace WBCs in UA can be seen with inflamed appendix adjacent to the ureter.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Visceral-to-somatic pain migration is pathognomonic for appendicitis
- Periumbilical → RLQ reflects T10 visceral afferents → localized parietal peritoneal inflammation
- Lying still = peritoneal irritation (movement worsens pain, unlike colicky pain where patients writhe)
- Positive psoas sign suggests retrocecal appendix position
- Trace pyuria without bacteria = reactive inflammation, not UTI

Evidence: Acute Appendicitis - StatPearls - Snyder MJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK493193. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Mesenteric lymphadenitis: Mesenteric lymphadenitis (viral) can mimic appendicitis but typically lacks the classic visceral-to-somatic pain migration, peritoneal signs are less pronounced, and WBC is often normal or mildly elevated. It is the most common appendicitis mimic in children.
- Right-sided diverticulitis: Right-sided diverticulitis is uncommon in young adults in Western populations (more common in Asian populations). It presents with RLQ pain but without the periumbilical-to-RLQ migration pattern. CT would show diverticular inflammation, not appendiceal enlargement.
- Meckel diverticulitis: Meckel diverticulum (rule of 2s: 2% of population, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long) can cause diverticulitis or bleeding. However, it typically presents in children <2 years with painless rectal bleeding (ectopic gastric mucosa), not the classic appendicitis pattern in a 24-year-old.
- Ureterolithiasis with referred pain: Kidney stones cause severe colicky flank pain radiating to the groin with hematuria. Patients with renal colic WRITHE in pain (unlike peritoneal irritation where patients lie still). UA shows hematuria, not trace WBCs only. The pain migration pattern and peritoneal signs are inconsistent with ureterolithiasis.

Reference: Appendicitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493193/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A medical student asks: "Why does appendicitis pain start around the umbilicus and then move to the right lower quadrant?" Which physiologic principle explains this classic pain migration?

choices.text: The appendix physically moves from the midline to the RLQ as it becomes inflamed
Visceral afferents from the distended appendix refer pain to the T10 dermatome (periumbilical), then parietal peritoneal inflammation localizes to the RLQ
Bacterial spread from the appendix to the periumbilical lymph nodes causes initial midline pain
Intestinal peristalsis pushes the inflammatory mediators centrally before they localize
The pain migration is a learned reporting pattern, not a true physiologic phenomenon

explanation:
Correct Answer: Visceral pain (poorly localized, midline) occurs when hollow viscera are distended, ischemic, or inflamed. Visceral afferents from the appendix travel with sympathetic nerves to T10 → perceived as periumbilical pain. When inflammation extends to the parietal peritoneum, somatic afferents (well-localized, sharp) produce precise RLQ pain. This visceral → somatic transition is a fundamental concept for all acute abdominal pathology.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Foregut organs (stomach, duodenum, liver, biliary) → T6-T9 → epigastric pain
- Midgut organs (appendix, small bowel, ascending colon) → T10-T11 → periumbilical pain
- Hindgut organs (descending colon, sigmoid, rectum) → T11-L1 → hypogastric/suprapubic pain
- Parietal peritoneum is innervated by somatic nerves → precise localization to the area of inflammation

Evidence: Abdominal Pain Physiology - StatPearls - Cartwright SL, Knudson MP. StatPearls. NBK459328. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- The appendix physically moves from the midline to the RLQ as it becomes inflamed: The appendix does not physically move. It remains in the RLQ (typically at McBurney's point). The pain migration reflects a change in the neural pathway being activated (visceral → somatic), not a change in organ position.
- Bacterial spread from the appendix to the periumbilical lymph nodes causes initial midline pain: The initial periumbilical pain is neural (visceral afferent referral), not caused by bacterial or lymphatic spread. Bacterial involvement is a late phenomenon (transmural infection → peritonitis), and lymph nodes play no role in the initial pain localization.
- Intestinal peristalsis pushes the inflammatory mediators centrally before they localize: This is not how abdominal pain works. Inflammatory mediators act locally. The periumbilical pain is caused by visceral afferent nerve referral to the T10 dermatome, not by physical movement of mediators.
- The pain migration is a learned reporting pattern, not a true physiologic phenomenon: The visceral-to-somatic transition is a well-established physiologic phenomenon based on dual innervation of abdominal organs. It has been validated in surgical studies and is a fundamental principle of abdominal pain evaluation.

Reference: Abdominal Pain - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459328/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 74-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation) presents with sudden severe diffuse abdominal pain. On initial exam, her abdomen is soft with minimal tenderness — the pain seems "out of proportion" to physical findings. 6 hours later, she develops rigid abdomen with peritoneal signs.Vital SignsBP 95/58HR 120RR 28Temp 37.8°CSpO₂ 94%Labs (6h)WBC 22.4 K/μLLactate 6.8 mmol/LBase deficit -12Hgb 14.2 g/dL What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute mesenteric ischemia (AMI): classic triad of (1) severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam, (2) atrial fibrillation or other embolic source, (3) rapid progression to peritonitis as bowel infarcts. SMA embolism is the most common cause (50%). Lactate elevation is a late finding indicating tissue necrosis. CT angiography is the diagnostic study of choice.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Pain out of proportion to exam = hallmark of mesenteric ischemia (early: mucosal ischemia without peritoneal inflammation)
- AF without anticoagulation = embolic risk; SMA embolism causes 50% of AMI
- 6-hour progression from soft abdomen to rigid/peritoneal signs = transmural infarction
- Lactate 6.8 with base deficit -12 = severe tissue ischemia and anaerobic metabolism
- Time-critical diagnosis: mortality >60% if diagnosis delayed beyond 24 hours

Evidence: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia - StatPearls - Bala M, et al. StatPearls. NBK431063. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Perforated peptic ulcer: Perforated peptic ulcer presents with sudden, severe epigastric pain with immediate board-like rigidity (chemical peritonitis from gastric acid). It does NOT start with pain out of proportion to exam — the exam is abnormal from the onset. CXR shows free air under diaphragm.
- Acute pancreatitis: Pancreatitis presents with constant epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea/vomiting, and elevated lipase. The abdomen is tender but not initially rigid. Pancreatitis doesn't typically cause the 'pain out of proportion to exam' pattern or rapid progression to peritonitis in 6 hours.
- Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm: Ruptured AAA presents with sudden back/flank/abdominal pain, hypotension, and a pulsatile abdominal mass. While it can present with peritonitis, the 'pain out of proportion to exam' pattern with AF source is more specific for mesenteric ischemia. AAA does not cause lactic acidosis from bowel necrosis.
- Small bowel obstruction with strangulation: SBO with strangulation can cause ischemia and peritonitis, but it presents with colicky pain, distension, vomiting, and obstipation — not the sudden onset of severe diffuse pain out of proportion to exam. The pattern of early pain with normal exam progressing to peritonitis is specific for mesenteric ischemia.

Reference: Mesenteric Ischemia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK431063/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with 8 hours of constant right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right scapula, nausea, and vomiting. She had similar but shorter episodes after fatty meals that resolved spontaneously. Temperature is 38.5°C.Vital SignsBP 135/80HR 92RR 18Temp 38.5°CSpO₂ 98%ExamMurphy sign Positive (inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation)RUQ Tender, no guardingLabsWBC 13.4 K/μLAST 52 U/LALT 48 U/LAlkPhos 142 U/LBilirubin 1.4 mg/dLLipase Normal What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute cholecystitis = cystic duct obstruction by gallstone → gallbladder distension → inflammation → infection. Key distinction from biliary colic: cholecystitis pain lasts >6 hours, is associated with fever, leukocytosis, and a positive Murphy sign. Biliary colic resolves within 6 hours without fever/WBC elevation. Ultrasound is first-line imaging (gallstones + gallbladder wall thickening >3 mm + pericholecystic fluid).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 8 hours of constant RUQ pain (>6h = beyond biliary colic duration)
- Fever + leukocytosis = inflammatory/infectious process (not present in simple colic)
- Positive Murphy sign is specific for cholecystitis
- Prior shorter episodes = prior biliary colic from transient cystic duct obstruction
- Mildly elevated AST/ALT/AlkPhos can occur from bile duct inflammation without obstruction

Evidence: Acute Cholecystitis - StatPearls - Gallaher JR, et al. StatPearls. NBK459171. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Biliary colic — self-limited episode: Biliary colic resolves within 6 hours and does NOT cause fever, leukocytosis, or a positive Murphy sign. This patient has 8 hours of pain with fever (38.5°C), WBC 13.4, and positive Murphy — all indicating inflammation beyond simple colic.
- Ascending cholangitis: Cholangitis = CBD obstruction + infection. Presents with Charcot's triad: fever + jaundice + RUQ pain. This patient has only mildly elevated bilirubin (1.4) without clinical jaundice, and no CBD dilation would be expected. Cholangitis is more toxic with higher fevers and sometimes Reynolds' pentad (+ AMS + hypotension).
- Gallstone pancreatitis: Gallstone pancreatitis presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back and significantly elevated lipase (≥3x normal). This patient's lipase is normal, and the pain is RUQ, not epigastric — making cholecystitis the diagnosis.
- Peptic ulcer disease: PUD presents with epigastric burning pain, often related to meals (worse after eating for gastric, improved then worse for duodenal). The RUQ location, positive Murphy sign, and gallstone risk factors point to biliary pathology.

Reference: Cholecystitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459171/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with fever (39.5°C), jaundice, and severe RUQ pain (Charcot's triad). She becomes hypotensive (BP 82/50) and confused during the ED evaluation.Vital SignsBP 82/50HR 118RR 24Temp 39.5°CSpO₂ 94%LabsWBC 24.2 K/μLBilirubin 8.4 mg/dL (direct 6.2)AST 320 U/LALT 285 U/LAlkPhos 520 U/LLipase Normal This patient has Reynolds' pentad. What is the critical intervention that antibiotics alone cannot replace?

choices.text: IV antibiotics escalation to meropenem and reassess in 24 hours

explanation:
Correct Answer: Reynolds' pentad = Charcot's triad (fever + jaundice + RUQ pain) + hypotension + altered mental status = suppurative (septic) cholangitis. This is a surgical emergency. Antibiotics treat infection but cannot resolve the obstruction. ERCP with biliary decompression (sphincterotomy, stone extraction, stent placement) is the definitive treatment and must be performed urgently.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- CBD obstruction + ascending infection = obstructed infected system → bacteremia → sepsis
- Antibiotics penetrate poorly into an obstructed biliary system
- ERCP achieves biliary decompression in >90% of cases
- Delay in biliary decompression increases mortality significantly
- Cholecystectomy addresses the gallbladder, not the CBD — wrong target for cholangitis

Evidence: Ascending Cholangitis - StatPearls - Gomi H, et al. StatPearls. NBK459182. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Emergency cholecystectomy: Cholecystectomy removes the gallbladder, not the CBD stone causing the obstruction. Cholangitis requires biliary DUCT decompression. Cholecystectomy should be performed after the acute cholangitis resolves to prevent recurrence, but it is not the emergent intervention needed.
- CT-guided percutaneous drainage of gallbladder: Percutaneous cholecystostomy drains the gallbladder, not the CBD. This is appropriate for acute cholecystitis in patients unfit for surgery, not for cholangitis. The problem is CBD obstruction requiring CBD decompression via ERCP.
- IV antibiotics escalation to meropenem and reassess in 24 hours: Antibiotics cannot sterilize an obstructed biliary system — the obstruction must be relieved. While broader antibiotics are appropriate if initial therapy fails, DELAYING biliary decompression by 24 hours in Reynolds' pentad significantly increases mortality. ERCP must happen urgently.
- TIPS placement for biliary decompression: TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) is used for portal hypertension complications (variceal bleeding, refractory ascites), not biliary obstruction. TIPS connects portal vein to hepatic vein — it has no role in CBD decompression.

Reference: Cholangitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459182/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 52-year-old woman with known gallstones presents with 4 hours of epigastric pain and new-onset jaundice. No fever. Ultrasound shows gallstones, a dilated CBD (10 mm), and no gallbladder wall thickening.LabsBilirubin 4.2 mg/dL (direct 3.1)AST 185 U/LALT 220 U/LAlkPhos 380 U/LWBC 8.2 K/μLLipase Normal What is the diagnosis, and what is the appropriate intervention?

choices.text: Biliary colic — supportive care and elective cholecystectomy
Choledocholithiasis (CBD stone) — ERCP for stone extraction before cholecystectomy
Gallstone pancreatitis — NPO, IV fluids, lipase monitoring
Mirizzi syndrome — magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography first

explanation:
Correct Answer: Choledocholithiasis: gallstone in the CBD causing biliary obstruction. Key findings: jaundice + dilated CBD (>6 mm, or >8 mm post-cholecystectomy) + elevated direct bilirubin and AlkPhos + transaminase elevation (from biliary epithelial pressure injury). No fever/WBC elevation distinguishes from cholangitis. Treatment: ERCP with stone extraction, followed by cholecystectomy.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Jaundice + dilated CBD (10 mm) + obstructive LFT pattern = biliary obstruction
- No fever + normal WBC = no infection (not cholangitis)
- Direct hyperbilirubinemia + elevated AlkPhos = obstructive/cholestatic pattern
- Normal lipase rules out pancreatitis
- ERCP is both diagnostic and therapeutic for CBD stones

Evidence: Choledocholithiasis - StatPearls - Konstantinos GC, et al. StatPearls. NBK441961. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Biliary colic — supportive care and elective cholecystectomy: Biliary colic does not cause jaundice, CBD dilation, or obstructive LFT elevations. The dilated CBD with hyperbilirubinemia indicates a stone in the CBD requiring ERCP, not just gallbladder management.
- Acute cholecystitis — early cholecystectomy: No gallbladder wall thickening, no fever, normal WBC — these rule out cholecystitis. The pathology is in the CBD (dilated to 10 mm with obstructive LFTs), not the gallbladder wall.
- Gallstone pancreatitis — NPO, IV fluids, lipase monitoring: Lipase is normal, which rules out pancreatitis. Gallstone pancreatitis presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back AND lipase ≥3x normal. While a CBD stone can cause both pancreatitis and biliary obstruction, this patient has obstruction without pancreatitis.
- Mirizzi syndrome — magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography first: Mirizzi syndrome is external compression of the common hepatic duct by an impacted cystic duct/gallbladder neck stone. While MRCP is useful for diagnosis, the clinical presentation (CBD dilation + obstructive labs) is more consistent with straightforward choledocholithiasis. MRCP is appropriate for intermediate-risk patients, but this patient is high-risk (CBD dilated + elevated bilirubin) warranting direct ERCP.

Reference: Choledocholithiasis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441961/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 48-year-old man with heavy alcohol use presents with sudden-onset severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting. He is in distress, leaning forward for relief. Abdomen is tender in the epigastrium with guarding but no rigidity.Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 108RR 22Temp 38.0°CSpO₂ 96%LabsLipase 1,240 U/L (normal Amylase 480 U/L (normal WBC 15.2 K/μLBUN 28 mg/dLGlucose 185 mg/dL What is the diagnosis, and which criterion is NOT part of the Atlanta classification for acute pancreatitis?

choices.text: Acute pancreatitis; diagnosis requires ALL three Atlanta criteria simultaneously
Acute pancreatitis; elevated WBC is a diagnostic criterion
Acute pancreatitis; diagnosis requires 2 of 3: typical pain + lipase ≥3x normal + characteristic imaging
Chronic pancreatitis exacerbation; lipase elevation confirms chronic disease
Alcoholic gastropathy; lipase can be elevated from gastric inflammation

explanation:
Correct Answer: Revised Atlanta Classification: acute pancreatitis requires 2 of 3 criteria: (1) Characteristic abdominal pain (severe, epigastric, radiating to back), (2) Serum lipase (or amylase) ≥3× upper limit of normal, (3) Characteristic findings on cross-sectional imaging. WBC, glucose, and BUN are prognostic markers (BISAP, Ranson), not diagnostic criteria. Lipase is preferred over amylase (more specific, longer elevation).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- This patient meets 2 of 3: classic pain + lipase ≥3x (1,240/60 = 20x)
- Imaging is NOT needed if first two criteria are met
- Lipase is more specific than amylase (amylase elevated in parotitis, macroamylasemia, renal failure)
- WBC, glucose, BUN predict severity (BISAP score) but are not diagnostic criteria
- Gallstones (#1 cause) and alcohol (#2 cause) account for 80% of acute pancreatitis

Evidence: Acute Pancreatitis - StatPearls - Forsmark CE, et al. StatPearls. NBK482468. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Acute pancreatitis; diagnosis requires ALL three Atlanta criteria simultaneously: Only 2 of 3 criteria are needed. Many patients with classic pain and markedly elevated lipase do not need imaging for diagnosis. CT is reserved for uncertain diagnosis, clinical deterioration, or evaluation of complications.
- Acute pancreatitis; elevated WBC is a diagnostic criterion: Elevated WBC is a marker of inflammation/severity (part of SIRS criteria and BISAP score) but is NOT one of the three Atlanta diagnostic criteria. Many conditions cause leukocytosis.
- Chronic pancreatitis exacerbation; lipase elevation confirms chronic disease: In chronic pancreatitis, the gland is burned out and may NOT elevate lipase/amylase significantly during flares (insufficient functional parenchyma). This patient's markedly elevated lipase (20x normal) with acute presentation is classic for ACUTE pancreatitis.
- Alcoholic gastropathy; lipase can be elevated from gastric inflammation: Lipase is specific to the pancreas (unlike amylase, which can be from salivary glands). A lipase ≥3x normal is essentially diagnostic of pancreatitis. Gastritis and gastropathy do not cause significant lipase elevation.

Reference: Acute Pancreatitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482468/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: The same patient (above) is found to have gallstones on ultrasound and a mildly dilated CBD (7 mm). Bilirubin is 2.8, ALT 180. No fever. He improves with IV fluids, and pain resolves by day 3. He is tolerating a regular diet. Before discharge, what intervention prevents the most dangerous recurrence?

choices.text: ERCP to clear possible CBD stone → then outpatient cholecystectomy in 6 weeks
Ursodeoxycholic acid for gallstone dissolution → elective cholecystectomy in 3 months
Discharge with GI follow-up; cholecystectomy only if recurrent episode
Sphincterotomy only — eliminates need for cholecystectomy

explanation:
Correct Answer: For gallstone pancreatitis: same-admission cholecystectomy after pancreatitis resolves is guideline-mandated. Delaying to outpatient increases recurrent gallstone pancreatitis risk to 25-30% within weeks. ERCP is indicated ONLY if cholangitis is present or there is persistent biliary obstruction. This patient's improving LFTs suggest the CBD stone has passed.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 25-30% recurrence of gallstone pancreatitis within weeks if cholecystectomy is delayed
- CBD 7 mm with improving bilirubin/ALT = stone likely passed spontaneously (common)
- ERCP not needed if: no cholangitis, no persistent biliary obstruction, improving LFTs
- Same-admission cholecystectomy is safe after acute inflammation resolves (usually day 3-5)
- AGA/ACG guidelines: index admission cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis

Evidence: Gallstone Pancreatitis - StatPearls - Tenner S, et al. StatPearls. NBK482468. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- ERCP to clear possible CBD stone → then outpatient cholecystectomy in 6 weeks: ERCP is NOT indicated when LFTs are improving (stone has likely passed). Unnecessary ERCP carries 5-10% complication rate (post-ERCP pancreatitis, perforation, bleeding). Additionally, outpatient cholecystectomy in 6 weeks allows a dangerous window for recurrence.
- Ursodeoxycholic acid for gallstone dissolution → elective cholecystectomy in 3 months: Ursodiol dissolves cholesterol stones slowly (6-24 months) with only 50% success rate. This does not prevent near-term recurrence. A 3-month delay to surgery exposes the patient to 25-30% risk of recurrent gallstone pancreatitis.
- Discharge with GI follow-up; cholecystectomy only if recurrent episode: Waiting for recurrence is dangerous — recurrent gallstone pancreatitis can be more severe and may be complicated by cholangitis, necrotizing pancreatitis, or death. Guidelines mandate same-admission cholecystectomy for precisely this reason.
- Sphincterotomy only — eliminates need for cholecystectomy: Endoscopic sphincterotomy reduces CBD stone recurrence but does NOT address the gallbladder as the source of stones. Without cholecystectomy, new stones continue to form and can cause biliary colic, cholecystitis, and recurrent pancreatitis. CCY is still needed.

Reference: Gallstone Pancreatitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482468/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis presents with increasing abdominal distension over 2 weeks. He has shifting dullness on exam. Diagnostic paracentesis is performed.Ascites FluidCell count WBC 180/μL (PMNs 35%)Albumin 0.8 g/dLTotal protein 1.2 g/dLGram stain NegativeCulture PendingSerumAlbumin 2.6 g/dL Calculate the SAAG and interpret the result.

choices.text: SAAG = 0.8 → low gradient → peritoneal carcinomatosis most likely
SAAG = 1.8 → high gradient (≥1.1) → portal hypertension is the cause of ascites
SAAG is not useful in alcoholic liver disease
Total protein >1 g/dL rules out portal hypertension

explanation:
Correct Answer: SAAG (serum-ascites albumin gradient) = serum albumin − ascites albumin. SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL = portal hypertension (97% accuracy): cirrhosis, heart failure, Budd-Chiari. SAAG : peritoneal carcinomatosis, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitic ascites. This patient: 2.6 − 0.8 = 1.8 (high gradient) = portal hypertension.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- SAAG = 2.6 (serum) − 0.8 (ascites) = 1.8 g/dL → high gradient
- High SAAG + known cirrhosis = portal hypertension as cause of ascites
- Ascites PMN count 63/μL (180 × 35%) → below 250/μL → NOT SBP (need PMN ≥250 for SBP)
- Low ascites total protein (<1.5 g/dL) = higher risk for SBP (low opsonization capacity)

Evidence: Ascites and SAAG - StatPearls - Runyon BA. StatPearls. NBK470482. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- SAAG = 0.8 → low gradient → peritoneal carcinomatosis most likely: Math error. SAAG = serum albumin (2.6) minus ascites albumin (0.8) = 1.8, not 0.8. The ascites albumin alone is 0.8, but that is not the SAAG.
- SAAG = 1.4 → intermediate → cannot determine etiology: There is no 'intermediate' SAAG. The cutoff is binary: ≥1.1 = portal hypertension, <1.1 = non-portal hypertensive. Additionally, the SAAG is 1.8, not 1.4.
- SAAG is not useful in alcoholic liver disease: SAAG is extremely useful in all forms of liver disease. It helps distinguish portal hypertensive ascites (cirrhosis, regardless of etiology) from other causes. It is one of the most validated tests in hepatology.
- Total protein >1 g/dL rules out portal hypertension: Ascites total protein does NOT determine the cause of ascites — SAAG does. Low ascites total protein (<1.5 g/dL) in cirrhotic ascites indicates higher SBP risk (low complement/opsonin levels) but does not change the SAAG interpretation.

Reference: Ascites - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470482/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 60-year-old man with cirrhosis and known ascites presents with fever, diffuse abdominal pain, and confusion. Diagnostic paracentesis shows: WBC 1,200/μL with PMN 820/μL (68%). Gram stain is negative.Vital SignsBP 92/55HR 112RR 22Temp 38.8°CSpO₂ 95% PMN count is 820/μL (≥250). What is the diagnosis and treatment?

choices.text: Secondary bacterial peritonitis — obtain CT abdomen and surgical consult
SBP — start IV cefotaxime (or ceftriaxone) + IV albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3)
Culture-negative neutrocytic ascites — wait for cultures before treating

explanation:
Correct Answer: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP): ascites PMN ≥250/μL in the absence of an intra-abdominal surgical source. Treat IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for cultures (culture-negative SBP is common). First-line: IV cefotaxime 2g q8h (or ceftriaxone 2g daily). Add IV albumin: 1.5 g/kg on day 1, 1 g/kg on day 3 — reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality (Sort RCT: mortality 10% vs 29%).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- PMN ≥250/μL + cirrhotic ascites = SBP (regardless of culture or gram stain)
- Most common organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus pneumoniae (monomicrobial)
- Negative gram stain does NOT exclude SBP — sensitivity of gram stain for SBP is only 10-15%
- IV albumin is CRITICAL: prevents renal failure in high-risk patients (Cr >1, BUN >30, bilirubin >4)
- After SBP resolution: lifelong SBP prophylaxis (norfloxacin 400 mg daily or TMP-SMX DS daily)

Evidence: SBP - StatPearls - Runyon BA. StatPearls. NBK470482. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Secondary bacterial peritonitis — obtain CT abdomen and surgical consult: Secondary peritonitis results from a perforated viscus (appendicitis, diverticulitis, perforated ulcer) and is polymicrobial. Clues: very high PMN (>10,000), multiple organisms on culture, elevated ascites LDH and protein, low ascites glucose. This patient has a lower PMN count consistent with SBP, not surgical pathology.
- SBP — start IV vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam: Correctly diagnoses SBP but uses the wrong empiric regimen. Third-generation cephalosporins (cefotaxime, ceftriaxone) are first-line and cover 90% of SBP organisms. Vancomycin + pip-tazo is unnecessarily broad and should be reserved for healthcare-associated SBP or prior multidrug-resistant organisms.
- Culture-negative neutrocytic ascites — wait for cultures before treating: NEVER wait for cultures. PMN ≥250/μL mandates immediate antibiotic treatment. Culture-negative neutrocytic ascites is TREATED THE SAME as culture-positive SBP — delay increases mortality.
- Tuberculous peritonitis — start RIPE therapy: TB peritonitis causes ascites with lymphocytic predominance (not neutrophilic), elevated ascites adenosine deaminase (ADA), and low SAAG. This patient has PMN-predominant ascites in cirrhosis, which is SBP. TB peritonitis is a different clinical entity.

Reference: SBP - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470482/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 62-year-old man with cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) is brought by family for increasing confusion over 3 days. He has asterixis on exam, is oriented only to self, and has a fetor hepaticus odor. He recently started hydrocodone for back pain and has not had a bowel movement in 5 days.Vital SignsBP 108/65HR 88RR 16Temp 37.0°CSpO₂ 97%LabsNH3 128 μmol/L (normal Cr 1.2 mg/dLNa 131 mEq/L What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment?

choices.text: Hepatic encephalopathy — start lactulose (titrate to 2-3 soft stools daily) + identify and treat
Hepatic encephalopathy — ammonia-lowering IV infusion (sodium benzoate)
Hepatic encephalopathy — start rifaximin alone without lactulose

explanation:
Correct Answer: Hepatic encephalopathy (HE): spectrum from subclinical to coma, caused by gut-derived neurotoxins (primarily ammonia) bypassing hepatic clearance. First-line: lactulose (titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day). Rifaximin is added for prevention of recurrence (not first-line monotherapy). ALWAYS identify precipitants: infection (SBP), GI bleeding, constipation, medications (opioids, benzodiazepines), dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Asterixis (liver flap) is pathognomonic for HE
- Ammonia 128 = markedly elevated (but serial ammonia levels are NOT useful for monitoring)
- Precipitants here: opioid (hydrocodone) + constipation (5 days no BM)
- Lactulose converts colonic ammonia (NH3) to ammonium (NH4+) which cannot be absorbed → fecal excretion
- Rifaximin reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria but is an ADD-ON to lactulose for recurrence prevention

Evidence: Hepatic Encephalopathy - StatPearls - Vilstrup H, et al. StatPearls. NBK441923. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Uremic encephalopathy — start hemodialysis: Creatinine is 1.2 (not elevated). Uremic encephalopathy requires significantly elevated BUN/Cr with renal failure features (asterixis can occur in uremia too, but in the context of cirrhosis + ammonia 128 + fetor hepaticus, HE is the diagnosis).
- Hepatic encephalopathy — ammonia-lowering IV infusion (sodium benzoate): Sodium benzoate and other ammonia scavengers (glycerol phenylbutyrate, L-ornithine-L-aspartate) are experimental or adjunctive therapies. Lactulose remains the established first-line treatment. IV sodium benzoate is not standard of care.
- Hepatic encephalopathy — start rifaximin alone without lactulose: Rifaximin is an ADD-ON to lactulose for secondary prevention, not first-line monotherapy for acute HE. The RFHE trial studied rifaximin added to lactulose, not rifaximin alone. Lactulose must be started first.
- Opioid-induced sedation — give naloxone: While the opioid is a precipitant contributing to HE, this is not simple opioid overdose. Asterixis, elevated ammonia, and fetor hepaticus confirm HE. Naloxone will not treat HE. However, the opioid should be discontinued as a precipitant management step.

Reference: Hepatic Encephalopathy - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441923/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 55-year-old woman with hepatitis C cirrhosis is being evaluated for liver transplant. Current labs: bilirubin 3.8, albumin 2.4, INR 1.8, creatinine 1.6, sodium 128. She has moderate ascites and Grade II encephalopathy (confusion, asterixis). Which scoring system determines her transplant priority, and what components does it use?

choices.text: Child-Pugh score using bilirubin, albumin, ascites, encephalopathy, and PT — determines transplant list priority
MELD-Na score using bilirubin, INR, creatinine, and sodium — determines organ allocation priority
APACHE II score — predicts ICU mortality
Glasgow-Blatchford score — determines need for intervention
Lille score — determines steroid responsiveness in alcoholic hepatitis

explanation:
Correct Answer: MELD-Na (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease with sodium) is used for transplant organ allocation priority in the US. Components: bilirubin, INR, creatinine, and sodium. Higher MELD = higher priority. Child-Pugh (bilirubin, albumin, ascites, encephalopathy, PT) classifies severity (A/B/C) and predicts prognosis but is NOT used for transplant listing.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- MELD-Na predicts 90-day mortality in cirrhosis and determines transplant priority
- MELD >15: transplant evaluation should begin; MELD >35-40: very high 90-day mortality
- Child-Pugh: older system, still useful for prognosis and surgical risk (Child C = highest risk)
- Key difference: MELD uses objective lab values only; Child-Pugh includes subjective components (ascites degree, encephalopathy grade)
- Sodium was added to MELD because hyponatremia is an independent predictor of mortality in cirrhosis

Evidence: MELD Score - StatPearls - Wiesner R, et al. StatPearls. NBK540901. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Child-Pugh score using bilirubin, albumin, ascites, encephalopathy, and PT — determines transplant list priority: Child-Pugh is still clinically useful for prognosis and surgical risk assessment, but it is NOT used for transplant organ allocation. MELD-Na replaced Child-Pugh for allocation because MELD uses objective lab values only (no subjective assessments of ascites or encephalopathy degree).
- APACHE II score — predicts ICU mortality: APACHE II is a general ICU severity score, not specific to liver disease. It is not used for transplant allocation.
- Glasgow-Blatchford score — determines need for intervention: Glasgow-Blatchford is used for upper GI bleeding risk stratification, not liver disease severity or transplant allocation.
- Lille score — determines steroid responsiveness in alcoholic hepatitis: The Lille score is used specifically in severe alcoholic hepatitis to assess response to corticosteroid therapy at day 7. It is not a transplant allocation tool. However, it is an important hepatology score to know.

Reference: MELD Score - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK540901/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 65-year-old woman with prior abdominal hysterectomy presents with 2 days of progressive crampy abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, and inability to pass flatus or stool for 24 hours. Abdomen is distended with high-pitched, tinkling bowel sounds.Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 96RR 20Temp 37.0°CSpO₂ 98%X-rayFindings Multiple dilated small bowel loops (>3 cm) with air-fluid levelsColon Decompressed, minimal colonic gas What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the #1 cause in this patient?

choices.text: Large bowel obstruction from colorectal cancer — colonoscopy needed
Paralytic ileus from electrolyte abnormality — check BMP
Small bowel obstruction from adhesions (#1 cause in patients with prior surgery)

explanation:
Correct Answer: Small bowel obstruction (SBO): colicky abdominal pain + vomiting + distension + obstipation (no flatus or stool). Adhesions from prior surgery are the #1 cause (60-75% of SBO). X-ray: dilated small bowel loops (>3 cm) with air-fluid levels and decompressed colon. CT with IV contrast is the definitive imaging study (identifies transition point and evaluates for strangulation).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Prior abdominal surgery = adhesion formation → mechanical obstruction of small bowel
- Classic tetrad: pain (colicky), vomiting, distension, obstipation
- Dilated small bowel + decompressed colon = obstruction at the small bowel level
- High-pitched tinkling bowel sounds = mechanical obstruction (vs absent sounds in ileus)
- CT: transition point, evaluates for strangulation (reduced wall enhancement, mesenteric haziness)

Evidence: Small Bowel Obstruction - StatPearls - Rami Reddy SR, Cappell MS. StatPearls. NBK441975. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Large bowel obstruction from colorectal cancer — colonoscopy needed: LBO shows dilated COLON (>6 cm, or cecum >9 cm) on X-ray. This patient has dilated SMALL bowel with a decompressed colon. Additionally, LBO presents with more gradual onset and later vomiting than SBO.
- Paralytic ileus from electrolyte abnormality — check BMP: Ileus presents with distension and absent/hypoactive bowel sounds (NOT high-pitched). Ileus affects both small and large bowel diffusely (gas throughout). This patient has high-pitched, tinkling sounds (mechanical obstruction) and small bowel specific dilation.
- Sigmoid volvulus — barium enema needed: Sigmoid volvulus shows a massively dilated sigmoid colon with 'coffee bean' sign on X-ray, not small bowel dilation. It occurs in elderly, institutionalized, or psychiatric patients. Initial treatment is endoscopic detorsion, not barium enema.
- Ogilvie syndrome (acute colonic pseudo-obstruction): Ogilvie syndrome is massive colonic dilation WITHOUT mechanical obstruction. It affects hospitalized patients, often post-surgical or critically ill. The colon (especially cecum) is dilated, but there is no small bowel obstruction pattern.

Reference: SBO - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441975/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A patient with SBO (above) is on conservative management (NPO, NGT, IV fluids) for 36 hours. She develops worsening pain now constant (no longer colicky), fever, and tachycardia. Abdominal exam shows localized peritoneal signs in the right lower quadrant.Vital SignsBP 98/60HR 118RR 24Temp 38.8°CSpO₂ 94%CT abdomenTransition point Distal ileumBowel wall enhancement Decreased in a 15-cm segmentMesenteric vessels Haziness, possible mesenteric fluidFree fluid Small amount in pelvis Which CT finding is most concerning, and what is the next step?

choices.text: Continue conservative management — 48 hours is not long enough for full trial
Administer IV Gastrografin challenge to promote resolution
Decreased bowel wall enhancement indicates strangulation — emergent surgical exploration
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe for 12 more hours
Percutaneous drain placement for the pelvic free fluid

explanation:
Correct Answer: Strangulation in SBO is indicated by: decreased or absent bowel wall enhancement on CT (ischemia), mesenteric haziness/fluid, change from colicky to constant pain, fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, and peritoneal signs. Strangulation requires emergent surgical exploration — delayed surgery increases mortality from 8% (viable bowel) to 25% (nonviable). Do NOT continue conservative management when strangulation is suspected.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Decreased bowel wall enhancement = compromised blood supply (strangulation/ischemia)
- Pain character change from colicky → constant = bowel ischemia (transmural inflammation)
- New fever + tachycardia + peritoneal signs = systemic inflammatory response to ischemic bowel
- Mesenteric haziness and free fluid = venous congestion and transudation from ischemic segment
- Every hour of delay increases risk of bowel necrosis, perforation, and death

Evidence: SBO Strangulation - StatPearls - Rami Reddy SR. StatPearls. NBK441975. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Continue conservative management — 48 hours is not long enough for full trial: The standard conservative trial is 48-72 hours for UNCOMPLICATED SBO. However, when signs of strangulation develop (decreased wall enhancement, peritoneal signs, hemodynamic changes), conservative management must be abandoned immediately. Continuing risks bowel necrosis and perforation.
- Administer IV Gastrografin challenge to promote resolution: Gastrografin challenge is used in partial, uncomplicated SBO to predict resolution and potentially promote bowel function through osmotic effects. It is CONTRAINDICATED when strangulation is suspected — it delays surgery and does not address ischemic bowel.
- Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe for 12 more hours: Antibiotics are appropriate (perioperatively) but they are NOT a substitute for surgical exploration. Antibiotics do not reverse bowel ischemia. Observing for 12 more hours allows the ischemic bowel to progress to necrosis and perforation.
- Percutaneous drain placement for the pelvic free fluid: The small amount of pelvic free fluid is a sign of strangulation (transudation from ischemic bowel), not an abscess requiring drainage. The treatment is surgical exploration to address the ischemic bowel, not drainage of a secondary finding.

Reference: SBO Strangulation - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441975/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: An 8-month-old previously healthy boy presents with episodic screaming and drawing up of his legs for the past 6 hours. Between episodes he appears lethargic. Parents report a "red currant jelly" stool in his diaper. Abdominal exam reveals a sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant.Vital SignsHR 160RR 36Temp 37.5°CSpO₂ 99% What is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?

choices.text: Intussusception — air or contrast enema reduction
Malrotation with midgut volvulus — emergent Ladd procedure

explanation:
Correct Answer: Intussusception: telescoping of proximal bowel into distal segment (most commonly ileocolic). Classic triad: (1) episodic colicky pain (screaming, drawing up legs), (2) currant jelly stool (blood + mucus from venous congestion), (3) sausage-shaped abdominal mass. Peak age: 6-18 months. First-line treatment: pneumatic (air) or hydrostatic (saline) enema reduction (success rate 80-95%). Surgical reduction if enema fails or perforation suspected.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Episodic pain with lethargy between episodes = hallmark of intussusception
- Currant jelly stool = mucosal ischemia from venous congestion of trapped bowel
- Sausage-shaped mass in RUQ = ileocolic intussusception (most common type)
- Ultrasound: 'target sign' or 'doughnut sign' on cross-section (concentric rings of bowel-in-bowel)
- Enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic — air enema preferred (less messy, similar success rate)

Evidence: Intussusception - StatPearls - Waseem M, et al. StatPearls. NBK431078. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Pyloric stenosis — pyloromyotomy: Pyloric stenosis presents at 3-6 weeks of age (not 8 months) with projectile NON-bilious vomiting, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, and palpable 'olive' mass in the epigastrium. There is no bloody stool or episodic pain.
- Meckel diverticulum — technetium-99m scan: Meckel diverticulum typically presents with painless rectal bleeding (ectopic gastric mucosa) in children <2 years. It does NOT cause episodic colicky pain, currant jelly stool, or sausage-shaped mass. However, Meckel can serve as a lead point for intussusception in older children.
- Malrotation with midgut volvulus — emergent Ladd procedure: Midgut volvulus presents with bilious vomiting (the hallmark) in a neonate or young infant. It is a surgical emergency. This patient has episodic colicky pain without bilious vomiting, and the sausage-shaped mass points to intussusception.
- Hirschsprung disease — rectal biopsy: Hirschsprung disease presents with failure to pass meconium in the first 48 hours, chronic constipation, abdominal distension, and enterocolitis. It does not cause episodic colicky pain or currant jelly stool.

Reference: Intussusception - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK431078/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 4-week-old firstborn male presents with 5 days of progressively forceful, projectile, NON-bilious vomiting after every feed. He is hungry immediately after vomiting. On exam, a firm, olive-shaped mass is palpated in the epigastrium.Vital SignsHR 170RR 38Temp 36.9°CWeight 3.2 kg (birth weight 3.5 kg)LabsNa 138 mEq/LK 3.0 mEq/LCl 88 mEq/LHCO₃ 32 mEq/LpH 7.52 What is the diagnosis, the expected metabolic abnormality, and the definitive treatment?

choices.text: GERD — positional therapy and thickened feeds
Pyloric stenosis — hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis — pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt)
Duodenal atresia — 'double bubble' sign — surgical repair

explanation:
Correct Answer: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis: hypertrophy of pyloric muscle causes gastric outlet obstruction. Classic: firstborn male, 3-6 weeks old, projectile non-bilious vomiting (obstruction is ABOVE the ampulla), palpable 'olive' mass, hungry after vomiting. Metabolic: hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis (loss of HCl in vomitus). Treatment: correct metabolic abnormalities FIRST, then Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Non-bilious = obstruction proximal to ampulla of Vater (pylorus or above)
- Bilious = obstruction DISTAL to ampulla (always concerning for malrotation)
- Projectile vomiting after every feed with immediate hunger = classic pyloric stenosis
- Olive mass = hypertrophied pylorus; palpable in 60-80% of cases
- Metabolic alkalosis: vomiting HCl → loss of H+ and Cl- → kidney compensates by retaining H+ and excreting K+ and HCO3-

Evidence: Pyloric Stenosis - StatPearls - Sola JE, et al. StatPearls. NBK537136. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- GERD — positional therapy and thickened feeds: GERD causes effortless spitting/regurgitation, not projectile vomiting. GERD does not cause metabolic alkalosis, an olive mass, or weight loss at this severity. The projectile nature and metabolic derangements indicate obstruction.
- Duodenal atresia — 'double bubble' sign — surgical repair: Duodenal atresia presents in the first days of life (not 4 weeks) with BILIOUS vomiting (obstruction distal to ampulla in most cases). X-ray shows 'double bubble' (dilated stomach + proximal duodenum). This patient has non-bilious vomiting at 4 weeks.
- Malrotation — bilious vomiting — Ladd procedure: The KEY word is NON-bilious. Malrotation with midgut volvulus causes BILIOUS (green) vomiting because the obstruction is distal to the ampulla of Vater. Non-bilious vomiting localizes the obstruction proximal to the ampulla (pylorus).
- Milk protein allergy — dietary elimination: Milk protein allergy can cause vomiting, but it also typically presents with diarrhea (often bloody), eczema, and irritability. It does not cause an olive mass, metabolic alkalosis, or projectile vomiting exclusively after feeds.

Reference: Pyloric Stenosis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537136/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 62-year-old man with the same risk factor profile presents with BURNING substernal chest discomfort that occurs after meals, especially when lying down. It is occasionally associated with an acid taste in his mouth. He tried antacids which provided temporary relief. He is concerned this is a heart attack. ECG is completely normal. Two serial troponins 6 hours apart are negative. He is hemodynamically stable.Vital SignsBP 134/82HR 76SpO2 98%WorkupECG Normal sinus rhythm, no ST changesTroponin x2 Negative (0.01, 0.01)CXR Normal With ACS ruled out, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what are the alarm features that would change the workup?

choices.text: ACS cannot be ruled out — admit for further monitoring
GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) — after ACS is reliably EXCLUDED (normal ECG + 2 negative troponins),
Esophageal spasm — causes crushing chest pain mimicking angina
Peptic ulcer disease — epigastric burning improved with eating
Chest wall pain — reproducible with palpation

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT from STEMI and PE: same patient, same chief complaint, but now the pain is BURNING (not crushing or pleuritic), POSTPRANDIAL, and relieved by ANTACIDS. ECG normal + troponin negative = ACS excluded. GERD: most common GI cause of non-cardiac chest pain (up to 60% of NCCP). Typical symptoms: heartburn (burning retrosternal pain), regurgitation (acid taste), water brash. Atypical: chronic cough, laryngitis, dental erosions, asthma exacerbation. Empiric PPI trial: omeprazole 20-40mg BID x 8 weeks; symptom resolution confirms diagnosis ("PPI test" sensitivity ~80%). Alarm features (RED FLAGS) requiring EGD: dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding/melena, anemia, persistent vomiting, new onset >60 years. Without alarm features: empiric PPI trial BEFORE endoscopy.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Burning quality (not crushing pressure or sharp pleuritic) = GI etiology
- Postprandial, worse lying down = positional reflux
- Acid taste = regurgitation (water brash)
- Relief with antacids = acid-mediated symptom
- Normal ECG + 2 negative troponins = ACS reliably excluded
- Hemodynamically stable, no respiratory distress = not PE
- No alarm features described = empiric PPI trial appropriate

Evidence: GERD - StatPearls - Richter JE, et al. StatPearls. NBK441938. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- ACS not ruled out: Two serial troponins 6 hours apart with a normal ECG have a negative predictive value >99% for ruling out ACS. The 0/1-hour or 0/3-hour high-sensitivity troponin protocols are designed to safely exclude MI. This patient's normal ECG, negative troponins, stable hemodynamics, and symptom quality (burning, postprandial, antacid-responsive) all point away from ACS.
- Esophageal spasm: Esophageal spasm (diffuse or nutcracker esophagus) can mimic angina with crushing chest pain and may even respond to NTG. However, this patient's pain is BURNING (not crushing), clearly postprandial, and relieved by antacids — all classic GERD features. Esophageal spasm is suspected when chest pain is non-burning and occurs with swallowing (dysphagia).
- Peptic ulcer disease: PUD causes EPIGASTRIC pain (not retrosternal), often described as gnawing/aching. Duodenal ulcer pain is classically RELIEVED by eating (pain 2-5 hours after meals, improved with food). Gastric ulcer pain is worsened by eating. This patient's symptoms are retrosternal, postprandial (worse after meals), and burning — more consistent with GERD.
- Chest wall pain: Musculoskeletal chest pain is REPRODUCIBLE with chest wall palpation and worsened by specific movements. It does NOT respond to antacids, is not postprandial, and does not cause acid taste. This patient's pain pattern (burning, postprandial, antacid-responsive) clearly points to GI etiology.

Reference: GERD - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441938/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 24-year-old woman has 4 months of RLQ pain, non-bloody diarrhea, 15-lb weight loss, and perianal pain. Colonoscopy shows SKIP LESIONS, cobblestoning, and deep linear ulcers. Biopsy shows non-caseating granulomas.ExamAbdomen RLQ massPerianal FistulaMouth Aphthous ulcers What distinguishes this from ulcerative colitis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Crohn vs UC: Skip lesions vs continuous; Transmural vs mucosal; Any GI tract vs colon; Fistulas vs none; Granulomas vs none; Perianal disease vs none.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Skip lesions = patchy = Crohn
- Granulomas = Crohn
- Perianal fistula = highly characteristic
- Mouth involvement = beyond colon

Evidence: Crohn Disease - StatPearls - Ranasinghe IR, et al. StatPearls. NBK436021. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- UC: UC is continuous from rectum, mucosal-only, no granulomas, no fistulas.
- Infectious: 4 months chronic, not acute.
- IBS: IBS doesn't cause weight loss or elevated calprotectin.
- Ischemic: Young patient, wrong location.

Reference: Crohn - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436021/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 22-year-old man presents with 18 hours of abdominal pain that began periumbilically and migrated to the RIGHT LOWER QUADRANT. He has anorexia, nausea, and low-grade fever. RLQ tenderness with guarding is present.Vital SignsTemp 38.2°CHR 96LabsWBC 14,200 (left shift)ExamMcBurney TenderRovsing Positive (RLQ pain with left-sided palpation)Psoas PositiveObturator Positive What clinical features are classic, and what is the management?

choices.text: CT abdomen is required in all cases before surgery
Antibiotics alone are sufficient for uncomplicated appendicitis
Pain starting in RLQ is more typical than migration

explanation:
Correct Answer: Appendicitis classic features:
• Pain migration: periumbilical → RLQ (visceral → parietal)
• Anorexia (nearly universal, absence should prompt reconsideration)
• Low-grade fever
• McBurney point tenderness, Rovsing, psoas, obturator signs
• Labs: leukocytosis with left shift

Alvarado score: Migration (1), Anorexia (1), Nausea (1), RLQ tenderness (2), Rebound (1), Fever (1), Leukocytosis (2), Left shift (1). ≥7 = high probability.

CT: >95% sensitivity, useful in atypical presentations. Ultrasound preferred in children and pregnancy. Classic presentation in young male can go directly to OR.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 18h of pain = appropriate timeline for appendicitis
- Periumbilical → RLQ migration = classic visceral to somatic
- Anorexia + nausea + low fever = typical
- WBC 14.2K with left shift = acute inflammation
- All physical signs positive = high probability
- Management: appendectomy + antibiotics

Evidence: Appendicitis - StatPearls - Snyder MJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK493193. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- CT always required: In a young male with CLASSIC presentation (migration, anorexia, McBurney point), CT is NOT required — can proceed to OR. CT is valuable for atypical presentations, women (rule out gyn causes), elderly, and immunocompromised.
- Antibiotics alone sufficient: Antibiotics-only management (studied in APPAC trial) has 70% initial success but 30-40% recurrence at 1 year. Appendectomy remains standard of care for uncomplicated appendicitis. Antibiotics alone may be considered in select cases or when surgery is high-risk.
- Pain starts in RLQ: MIGRATION is the classic feature. Pain begins PERIUMBILICALLY (visceral pain from T10 dermatome) then localizes to RLQ when parietal peritoneum is irritated. Pain starting in RLQ is atypical.
- Anorexia uncommon: Anorexia is nearly UNIVERSAL in appendicitis — its ABSENCE should raise doubt about the diagnosis. The triad is classically pain → anorexia → nausea/vomiting (in that order).

Reference: Appendicitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493193/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 45-year-old obese woman presents with 8 hours of severe constant RIGHT UPPER QUADRANT pain radiating to her right scapula. Pain began after a fatty meal. She has nausea and vomiting. Murphy sign is positive (inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation).Vital SignsTemp 38.4°CHR 102LabsWBC 15,800ALT 52AST 48Alk Phos 142Bilirubin 1.4RUQ UltrasoundFindings Gallstones, thickened gallbladder wall (5mm), pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign positive What findings confirm the diagnosis, and what is the timing of surgery?

choices.text: HIDA scan is required before surgery
Delay surgery until inflammation resolves (6-8 weeks)
Bilirubin 1.4 indicates choledocholithiasis requiring ERCP first

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT from appendicitis: Both cause acute abdominal pain with fever and leukocytosis, but:
• RUQ (not RLQ)
• Radiates to SCAPULA (phrenic nerve irritation) vs none
• POSTPRANDIAL (fatty food trigger) vs unrelated to eating
• MURPHY sign vs McBurney point
• Gallstones on US vs normal US

Tokyo Guidelines severity:
• Mild: no organ dysfunction, mild inflammation
• Moderate: WBC >18K, mass, symptoms >72h
• Severe: organ dysfunction (hypotension, altered mental status, renal/hepatic dysfunction)

Timing: cholecystectomy within 72 hours — early surgery decreases total hospital stay, complications, and cost vs delayed surgery.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- RUQ pain + postprandial + fatty food = biliary colic origin
- Fever + leukocytosis = inflammation (not simple biliary colic)
- Murphy sign positive = classic
- US: stones + wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid = cholecystitis
- Mild LFT elevation = gallbladder inflammation, not CBD obstruction
- Bilirubin 1.4 = mildly elevated but not obstructive pattern
- Management: antibiotics + cholecystectomy within 72h

Evidence: Cholecystitis - StatPearls - Zakko SF, et al. StatPearls. NBK430773. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- HIDA scan required: HIDA scan is reserved for EQUIVOCAL cases when US is non-diagnostic. This patient has classic findings: RUQ pain, fever, Murphy sign, AND confirmatory US (stones, wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid). HIDA adds nothing here.
- Antibiotics alone: Antibiotics treat infection but do NOT remove the obstructing stone or prevent recurrence. Cholecystectomy is definitive treatment. Antibiotics alone are only for patients too unstable for surgery, combined with percutaneous drainage.
- Delay surgery 6-8 weeks: This was historical practice but is now WRONG. Multiple RCTs show early cholecystectomy (<72h) is superior: shorter hospital stay, fewer complications, lower cost. Delayed surgery allows inflammation to organize, making dissection harder.
- ERCP first for bilirubin 1.4: Bilirubin 1.4 is only MILDLY elevated and can occur with gallbladder inflammation alone. Choledocholithiasis (CBD stone) typically causes bilirubin >4, dilated CBD on US, and significant alk phos elevation. This patient has gallbladder disease, not CBD obstruction. ERCP is for CBD stones.

Reference: Cholecystitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430773/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old man presents with 1 week of jaundice, fatigue, nausea, and RUQ discomfort. He recently returned from a mission trip to Central America where he drank local water. He has no history of alcohol use or IV drug use. Labs show markedly elevated transaminases.LabsAST 2,840 U/L (normal ALT 3,120 U/L (normal Alk_Phos 142 U/L (mildly elevated)Total bilirubin 8.4 mg/dLDirect bilirubin 6.2 mg/dL (DIRECT hyperbilirubinemia)INR 1.3Albumin 3.8 g/dL What pattern of liver injury is this, and what is the most likely etiology?

choices.text: Chronic hepatitis C with acute flare

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT: Hepatocellular vs hemolytic jaundice

| Feature | Hemolytic | Hepatocellular |
|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Bilirubin | INDIRECT | DIRECT |
| AST/ALT | Normal | Markedly elevated |
| LDH | Elevated (RBC source) | Elevated (liver source) |
| Haptoglobin | Low/absent | Normal |
| Reticulocytes | Elevated | Normal |

Hepatocellular injury patterns:
• AST/ALT >1000: viral, drug-induced, ischemic, autoimmune
• AST > ALT (2:1): alcoholic hepatitis
• ALT > AST: viral, NAFLD

Hepatitis A: fecal-oral, travel/outbreak, self-limited, no chronic infection. Best initial test: IgM anti-HAV.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Jaundice + elevated transaminases = liver disease
- AST/ALT >1000 = acute hepatocellular injury
- Direct hyperbilirubinemia = hepatic cause (not hemolysis)
- Travel to Central America + contaminated water = HAV risk
- No alcohol, no IVDU = less likely HCV, alcoholic hepatitis
- INR 1.3 = mild coagulopathy (monitor for fulminant)
- Self-limited but can rarely cause acute liver failure

Evidence: Hepatitis A - StatPearls - Matheny SC, et al. StatPearls. NBK459290. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Hemolytic anemia: Hemolytic jaundice causes INDIRECT hyperbilirubinemia (unconjugated) with NORMAL transaminases. This patient has DIRECT hyperbilirubinemia with AST/ALT >3000 — clear hepatocellular injury. The bilirubin pattern immediately distinguishes hemolytic (indirect) from hepatocellular (direct) jaundice.
- Cholestatic obstruction: Cholestatic pattern has alkaline phosphatase elevated >3x normal with relatively mild transaminase elevation (opposite of this case). This patient has AST/ALT >1000 with alk phos only mildly elevated (142) — hepatocellular pattern, not cholestatic. Cholestasis would also show dilated bile ducts on imaging.
- Alcoholic hepatitis: Alcoholic hepatitis typically shows AST:ALT ratio >2:1 (usually 2-3:1), and transaminases rarely exceed 300-500 U/L. This patient has ALT > AST and transaminases >3000 — not alcoholic pattern. Also, he has no alcohol history.
- Chronic HCV flare: Acute exacerbation of chronic HCV can cause elevated transaminases, but HCV transmission is parenteral (IVDU, blood products), not fecal-oral. The patient has no risk factors for HCV and has classic HAV exposure (travel to endemic area, contaminated water). HCV is also more chronic and insidious.

Reference: Hepatitis A - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459290/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of progressive painless jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and 15-lb weight loss. He has no abdominal pain. He reports pruritus. On exam, the gallbladder is palpable.LabsTotal bilirubin 14.2 mg/dLDirect bilirubin 12.8 mg/dL (>50% = DIRECT)AST 68ALT 72Alk phos 482 (markedly elevated, normal GGT 358 (elevated)CA_19_9 842 (elevated)ImagingRUQ_US Dilated intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts, CBD 12mm (normal CT_abdomen Mass in head of pancreas, dilated biliary tree What pattern of jaundice is this, and what is the likely diagnosis?

choices.text: OBSTRUCTIVE (cholestatic) jaundice from PANCREATIC HEAD CANCER —

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT: Obstructive vs other jaundice

Cholestatic/obstructive pattern:
• Alk phos markedly elevated (>3x normal)
• GGT elevated (confirms hepatobiliary source of alk phos)
• Transaminases mildly elevated
• DIRECT hyperbilirubinemia
• Dilated bile ducts on imaging

Courvoisier's Law: painless jaundice + palpable gallbladder = likely MALIGNANT obstruction (not stones). Chronic stone disease → scarred, fibrotic GB that can't distend.

Painless vs painful jaundice:
• Painless + weight loss: malignancy (pancreatic, cholangiocarcinoma, ampullary)
• Painful (colicky): choledocholithiasis
• Painful + fever: cholangitis

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Progressive painless jaundice + weight loss = malignancy
- Pale stools + dark urine = obstructed bile flow
- Palpable gallbladder = Courvoisier sign (malignant obstruction)
- Alk phos 482 = cholestatic pattern (not hepatocellular)
- Direct bili 12.8/14.2 = mostly conjugated (obstructive)
- Dilated CBD 12mm + pancreatic head mass = pancreatic cancer
- CA 19-9 elevated = supports pancreatic malignancy

Evidence: Pancreatic Cancer - StatPearls - McGuigan A, et al. StatPearls. NBK518996. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Hepatocellular injury: Hepatocellular injury (hepatitis) shows AST/ALT markedly elevated (often >1000) with relatively normal alk phos. This patient has the OPPOSITE pattern: alk phos 482 (markedly elevated) with AST/ALT only 68/72 (mildly elevated). This is CHOLESTATIC/obstructive pattern, not hepatocellular.
- Hemolytic jaundice: Hemolytic jaundice causes INDIRECT hyperbilirubinemia with normal transaminases and alk phos. This patient has DIRECT hyperbilirubinemia (12.8/14.2) with elevated alk phos — obstructive pattern. Also, hemolysis wouldn't cause pale stools, dilated bile ducts, or weight loss.
- Primary biliary cholangitis: PBC causes cholestatic jaundice but is a chronic autoimmune disease of intrahepatic bile ducts, primarily in middle-aged women. It presents with fatigue, pruritus, and gradual progression over years. This patient has ACUTE onset, a pancreatic head mass, weight loss, and palpable gallbladder — malignant obstruction, not PBC.
- Choledocholithiasis: CBD stones cause PAINFUL biliary colic, not painless progressive jaundice. Also, Courvoisier's Law states that a palpable gallbladder with jaundice suggests MALIGNANCY (not stones) because chronic stone disease scars the gallbladder, preventing distension. This patient's palpable GB and painless jaundice strongly suggest malignancy.

Reference: Pancreatic Cancer - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK518996/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 62-year-old man presents with 25-lb unintentional weight loss over 4 months. Unlike the previous patient, his appetite has DECREASED. He reports fatigue, change in bowel habits (alternating constipation and diarrhea), and occasional rectal bleeding. He has never had a colonoscopy. Family history: father had colon cancer at 58.LabsHgb 9.2 (low)MCV 72 (microcytic)Ferritin 8 (low — iron deficiency)FOBT Positive (occult blood)CEA 12.8 (elevated, normal What type of anemia is this, and what workup is required?

choices.text: Start iron supplementation and recheck in 3 months
COLON CANCER presenting with iron deficiency anemia and weight loss — IRON DEFICIENCY
Depression causing weight loss and decreased appetite

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT: Weight loss + DECREASED appetite suggests malignancy
(vs hyperthyroidism: weight loss + INCREASED appetite)

Iron deficiency anemia + age >45 = COLONOSCOPY:
• Right colon cancer: slow occult bleeding → IDA
• Left colon cancer: obstruction → change in caliber
• Rectal: visible blood, tenesmus

Colon cancer screening (USPSTF 2021):
• Average risk: start at 45 (colonoscopy q10y, or other modalities)
• Family hx: start 10 years before earliest diagnosis or age 40, whichever first

CEA: NOT for screening (poor sensitivity); used for monitoring treatment response and recurrence.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Unintentional weight loss + decreased appetite = malignancy red flag
- 62-year-old male = age risk for colorectal cancer
- Father with colon cancer at 58 = increased risk
- Iron deficiency anemia (MCV 72, ferritin 8) = GI blood loss
- Positive FOBT = occult bleeding
- Change in bowel habits = concerning in >45
- COLONOSCOPY is mandatory — cannot just treat the iron deficiency

Evidence: Colorectal Cancer - StatPearls - Rawla P, et al. StatPearls. NBK470380. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Iron supplementation only: Treating iron deficiency WITHOUT investigating the cause is DANGEROUS in adults >45. Iron deficiency anemia in this age group is GI blood loss until proven otherwise. Supplementation may temporarily improve hemoglobin but delays diagnosis of a potentially curable cancer.
- Celiac disease: Celiac can cause iron malabsorption and weight loss, but typically presents with diarrhea (not alternating bowel habits), and celiac does NOT cause positive FOBT or elevated CEA. The age, family history, and occult blood make colon cancer far more likely and must be excluded first.
- Depression: Depression can cause decreased appetite and weight loss, but does NOT cause iron deficiency anemia, microcytosis, or positive FOBT. These objective findings indicate organic pathology (blood loss) requiring investigation.
- IBD flare: IBD can cause weight loss and change in bowel habits, but typically presents in younger patients (15-30 years), has chronic relapsing course, and elevated inflammatory markers. This patient's new-onset symptoms at 62 with IDA and family history of CRC requires colonoscopy to rule out malignancy first.

Reference: Colorectal Cancer - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470380/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 48-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back for 12 hours, worse when supine and improved by sitting forward. He has been vomiting and unable to eat.Vital SignsTemp 38.2°CHR 112BP 102/68LabsLipase 1,840 U/L (elevated, normal WBC 14,200Glucose 188LDH 420AST 62Cr 1.6Ca 8.2 What criteria are used for severity assessment, and what is the initial management?

choices.text: CT abdomen immediately to assess for necrosis
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with lactated Ringer's, pain control, and NPO initially —
Start prophylactic antibiotics for pancreatic necrosis prevention
Emergent ERCP for all cases of acute pancreatitis
Morphine is contraindicated for pain — use meperidine instead

explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute pancreatitis diagnosis: 2 of 3 criteria (1) characteristic epigastric pain radiating to back, (2) lipase >3x normal, (3) imaging findings. Initial management: (1) AGGRESSIVE IV FLUIDS — LR preferred (5-10 mL/kg/hr initially, goal UOP 0.5 mL/kg/hr), (2) pain control (any opioid — morphine is NOT contraindicated despite historical teaching), (3) NPO initially, but EARLY oral feeding (within 24-48h) improves outcomes, (4) CT is NOT needed at admission — reserve for non-improvement at 48-72h or suspected complications. Severity: Ranson criteria (≥3 at 48h = severe), BISAP score, APACHE II. This patient has several poor prognostic markers: WBC >16K, glucose >200, LDH >350, elevated Cr, low Ca. Prophylactic antibiotics NOT recommended (no benefit, promotes resistance). ERCP: only for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis or persistent obstruction.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Epigastric pain radiating to back + lipase 1840 = acute pancreatitis
- Alcohol use = most common cause (#2 after gallstones globally)
- Tachycardia + mild hypotension = volume depletion
- Aggressive LR resuscitation = most important initial step
- Pain control with any opioid (morphine is safe)
- No CT needed on admission — clinical diagnosis
- Multiple poor prognostic markers → likely severe

Evidence: Acute Pancreatitis - StatPearls - Vege SS, et al. AGA Guidelines. Gastroenterology. 2018.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Immediate CT: CT at admission adds little to management of acute pancreatitis and cannot detect necrosis in the first 48-72 hours. CT is reserved for patients who fail to improve or are suspected of developing complications (necrosis, pseudocyst, abscess).
- Prophylactic antibiotics: Multiple RCTs and meta-analyses show prophylactic antibiotics do NOT prevent infected necrosis and promote antibiotic resistance. Antibiotics are reserved for documented infected necrosis (gas in pancreas on CT, positive FNA culture).
- Emergent ERCP: ERCP is indicated ONLY for gallstone pancreatitis with concurrent cholangitis (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) or persistent biliary obstruction. It is NOT indicated for alcoholic pancreatitis or uncomplicated gallstone pancreatitis.
- Meperidine instead of morphine: The historical claim that morphine causes sphincter of Oddi spasm and worsens pancreatitis is NOT supported by evidence. ALL opioids affect the sphincter of Oddi. Meperidine has seizure risk (normeperidine accumulation) and is NOT preferred. Use any appropriate opioid for pain control.

Reference: Acute Pancreatitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482468/ [Accessed 2025]
